title: Smoking cessation
content:
Here is the lecture content fully reorganized into a clean, exam-ready study guide for the NDECC SJ (Situational Judgement) exam.

***

# NDECC SJ: Smoking Cessation – Study Guide

## Recognizing the Station Type

In an SJ station, a prompt may seem to overlap two domains (e.g., health promotion **and** a medical questionnaire). Don't get confused — focus on what action is required. In this case:[1]
- The **desire to quit smoking** = Health Promotion task
- The **medical questionnaire** = History-taking duty

Both must be addressed, but the **primary driver** is smoking cessation counselling.[2]

***

## Step 1: Opening & Identify Smoking Status

Begin with a brief, empathetic opening (introduce yourself, confirm patient's desire to quit), then ask targeted questions:[3]

- How long have you been smoking? *(e.g., 15 years)*
- How many cigarettes per day? *(e.g., 14–15 cpd)*
- What type of tobacco? *(e.g., cigarettes)*
- Have you ever tried to quit before? *(e.g., tried chewing gum — no nicotine, so ineffective)*

***

## Step 2: Stages of Change — Assessing Readiness

This determines how deeply you discuss NRT:[4]

| Stage | Patient Response | Your Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Contemplation** | Wants to quit within 6 months, but not 1 month | Supportive listening; avoid overwhelming with NRT details |
| **Preparation** | Wants to quit within the **next 1 month** | ✅ Begin full NRT discussion |
| **Ready (Action)** | Wants to quit immediately / right away | Full NRT counselling + set quit date |

> **Key rule:** Only start detailed NRT counselling when the patient is in the **preparation or action phase** (within 1 month).[4]

***

## Step 3: Medical History — Mandatory Duty

Even when the focus is health promotion, the medical questionnaire must be reviewed. For **this case**, flag and follow up on:[5]

1. **Allergies** — Patient marked "yes" but listed no specific allergen → ask what they are allergic to
2. **Asthma** — Diagnosed a few years ago, reports it is getting worse → ask about severity, current management
3. **Breathing problems** — Also marked "yes" (patient treats it as separate from asthma) → clarify if related

**Clinical link to smoking:** Reinforce that quitting will **not cure asthma**, but continuing to smoke will very likely **worsen** both asthma and breathing difficulties. Use this as a motivator.[6]

***

## Step 4: NRT Counselling (3-Part Approach)

### A. Assess Past Attempts & Lessons Learned
- Ask: *"Have you tried anything to quit before?"*
- Patient tried chewing gum → it failed because regular gum has **no nicotine** — body still experiences full withdrawal[7]

### B. Identify Barriers to Quitting
Common barriers to explore:[3]
- Social smoking (e.g., group at work during breaks)
- Stress-driven smoking
- Ingrained habit (e.g., 15+ years — this patient's case)

### C. Provide NRT Information & Resources
The **ODA/CDA recommends** dental clinics stock NRT materials and the entire team should be educated about cessation options.[4]

***

## NRT Products (OTC — Canada)

| Product | How It Works | Tapering Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Nicotine Patch** | Sustained-release nicotine absorbed through skin; worn on arm/torso | Start: change every 2 days → gradually extend intervals between changes |
| **Nicotine Gum (Lozenge)** | Use whenever urge to smoke arises; replaces each smoking urge | Start at ~15 pieces/day → reduce over weeks/months; **use 4 mg if >25 cpd, 2 mg if <25 cpd** [5] |

**Key rationale for NRT:** After 15 years of smoking, the body cannot tolerate a sudden crash in nicotine. NRT tapers the body off gradually, **reducing withdrawal symptoms** and improving quit compliance.[7]

***

## Step 5: Build the Action Plan

1. **Set a quit date** — involve the patient; a reasonable target is ~4–6 months
2. **Choose NRT product** together (patch vs. gum based on preference)
3. **Schedule follow-up** — e.g., review in 1 week
4. **Offer booklets/resources** on NRT (clinic should have these available)[4]
5. **Reinforce motivation** — link quitting to better control of asthma & breathing

***

## Scope of Practice Boundary

As a **dentist**, your role is: **Identification → Education → Support**.[4]

- ✅ OTC NRT: patches, gum, lozenges — you can recommend these
- ❌ Prescription-based medications (e.g., Varenicline/Champix, Bupropion) — **requires a CE/pharmacotherapy course OR refer to physician**[4]

***

## Quick Exam Checklist

- [ ] Open station: introduce + confirm reason for visit
- [ ] Smoking history: duration, cpd, tobacco type, prior quit attempts
- [ ] Assess readiness to quit: contemplation vs. preparation/action
- [ ] Address medical questionnaire: allergies, asthma, breathing problems
- [ ] Link smoking → worsening asthma (motivational hook)
- [ ] Explain NRT rationale (why gum alone failed)
- [ ] Present NRT options: patch + nicotine gum/lozenge
- [ ] Set action plan with quit date + follow-up
- [ ] Stay within scope; refer to physician if prescription meds needed

Sources
[1] Situational Judgement Preparation Course For NDECC https://www.itdonline.ca/courses/situational-judgement-for-ndecc
[2] Everything about NDEB's NDECC situational judgement exam https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ymjsBR2-occ
[3] Findings and Future Directions from a Smoking Cessation ... https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11581356/
[4] A Practical Guide to Tobacco Cessation in Dental Offices https://www.cda-adc.ca/jcda/vol-67/issue-3/153.pdf
[5] Dispensing of Nicotine Replacement Therapy (NRT) https://www.youcanmakeithappen.ca/wp-content/uploads/2014/10/DirectiveDispensingNRTNov2011Final.pdf
[6] Smoking cessation https://www.dentaljuce.com/smoking-cessation-quitting-smoking
[7] Nicotine Replacement Therapy (NRT) Guidance for Patients https://www.pharmacists.ca/cpha-ca/assets/File/smoking-cessation/Nicotine-Replacement-Therapy-NRT-Guidance-for-Patients_EN2.pdf
[8] NDECC​® - The National Dental Examining Board of ... https://ndeb-bned.ca/equivalency-process/ndecc-2/

[10] [PDF] TCFHT-MD10_Nicotine Replacement Therapy - MEDICAL DIRECTIVE https://taddlecreekfht.ca/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/TCFHT-MD10_Nicotine-Replacement-Therapy-2024-06-14-FINAL.pdf
[11] Home https://jcnde.ada.org

Here is a full, detailed dentist–patient dialogue script structured around the **5 A's framework** — the gold standard for tobacco cessation in dental practice. Use this for active recall and mock practice.[1][2]

***

# NDECC SJ Script: Smoking Cessation Station

## Station Context
> **Scenario:** A patient has filled in a medical questionnaire at your dental clinic. They have indicated a desire to quit smoking. They also marked **yes** to: currently under physician care, taking medications (unlisted), allergies (unlisted), asthma, and breathing problems. They marked **yes** to tobacco use.

***

## Opening — Establish Rapport

> **Dentist:** "Good morning! My name is Dr. [Name], and I'll be your dentist today. Before we get started, I just want to take a few minutes to go over what you've filled in on your questionnaire and have a chat with you. Is that okay?"

> **Patient:** "Yes, of course."

> **Dentist:** "Wonderful. I noticed you mentioned that you'd like to quit smoking — first of all, I really want to commend you for that. That takes courage and it's a really positive step for your overall health. I'd love to support you in that journey today."

***

## The 5 A's in Action

### A1 — ASK: Establish Smoking History

> **Dentist:** "So I can better understand where you're at, could you tell me a little more about your smoking history? How long have you been smoking?"

> **Patient:** "About 14 to 15 years now."

> **Dentist:** "Thank you for sharing that. And roughly how many cigarettes would you say you smoke in a day?"

> **Patient:** "Around 14 to 15 cigarettes a day."

> **Dentist:** "Got it. And I can see on the questionnaire you marked tobacco use — just to be specific, is it cigarettes, or are you using any other form of tobacco such as a pipe or chewing tobacco?"

> **Patient:** "Just cigarettes."

> **Dentist:** "Perfect, thank you. That really helps me understand your situation better."

***

### A2 — ADVISE: Personalized, Clear Message

> **Dentist:** "I do want to take a moment to share with you — and I'll touch on this more in a little bit — that smoking, especially over a long period like yours, can have quite a significant impact on your overall and oral health. The great news is that making a change, even at this stage, can make a real difference. So I'm genuinely glad you've decided to take this step."

***

### A3 — ASSESS: Readiness to Quit (Stages of Change)

> **Dentist:** "Now, I'd love to know a little more about where you are mentally with quitting. Have you ever tried to quit smoking before?"

> **Patient:** "Yes, actually. I tried chewing gum for a while, but it just didn't work. After every piece of gum I still had this huge urge to smoke."

> **Dentist:** "That's a really common experience, and it makes a lot of sense — we'll come back to why that happened in just a moment. Now, in terms of timing — when are you thinking about quitting? Is this something you're considering within the next six months?"

> **Patient:** "Yes, definitely within six months."

> **Dentist:** "That's great. And would you say you're looking at doing this within the next month, or even sooner?"

> **Patient:** "Yes, I want to quit as soon as possible — ideally within the next month."

> **Dentist:** "Excellent. That tells me you are absolutely ready to have this conversation, and I'm very happy to help you put together a real plan today."

> *(Internal note: Patient is in the **Preparation Phase** → proceed with full NRT discussion)*[3]

***

## Medical History Review (Questionnaire Duty)

> **Dentist:** "Before we dive deeper into the cessation plan, I do want to go through a few things on your questionnaire, just to make sure I have a complete picture of your health. You mentioned you're currently under a physician's care — could you tell me what you're seeing your physician for?"

> **Patient:** "Just general health checkups."

> **Dentist:** "Wonderful. And you did mention you're taking some medications — could you let me know what those are? Even vitamins or over-the-counter things count."

> **Patient:** "[Patient lists medications]."

> **Dentist:** "Thank you. Now, you also indicated that you have some allergies — could you tell me specifically what you're allergic to?"

> **Patient:** "I think it's a reaction to a certain shampoo or a face wash. Nothing serious."

> **Dentist:** "Good to know, thank you. That helps me make sure we use the right products here at the clinic."

> **Dentist:** "I also noticed you marked yes for both asthma and breathing problems. Could you tell me a little more about your asthma — when were you diagnosed, and how have things been going?"

> **Patient:** "I was diagnosed a few years ago. Honestly, it feels like it's been getting worse lately."

> **Dentist:** "I'm sorry to hear that. And the breathing problems you listed — are those connected to your asthma, or do you feel they're separate issues?"

> **Patient:** "I think they might be related, but they feel different to me sometimes."

> **Dentist:** "That's a very important observation, and I really appreciate you flagging both. I want to make a connection here — while quitting smoking won't make your asthma disappear overnight, continuing to smoke significantly increases the risk of your asthma and breathing difficulties getting worse over time. So your decision to quit is actually one of the best things you can do for your lungs right now."[3]

***

### A4 — ASSIST: NRT Education & Barrier Identification

> **Dentist:** "Now, let's circle back to your previous quit attempt. You mentioned you tried chewing gum — regular gum, correct?"

> **Patient:** "Yes, just regular gum."

> **Dentist:** "That explains why it didn't work. Regular chewing gum doesn't contain nicotine. After 15 years of smoking, your body has become physically dependent on nicotine. When you stop suddenly, you experience strong withdrawal symptoms — cravings, irritability, anxiety — which is what pushes people back to smoking. What we want to do is taper your body off nicotine gradually, so those withdrawal symptoms are manageable."[4]

> **Dentist:** "This approach is called **Nicotine Replacement Therapy, or NRT**. Have you heard of it before?"

> **Patient:** "I've heard the term but I'm not sure exactly how it works."

> **Dentist:** "Let me explain the two main options available over the counter here in Canada. The first is the **nicotine patch**. You apply it directly to your skin — on your arm or torso — and it releases nicotine slowly and steadily throughout the day. You wear it for about two to three days before replacing it, and over time, we gradually increase the gap between patch changes so your body slowly gets used to less and less nicotine."[4]

> **Dentist:** "The second option is **nicotine gum or lozenges**. Unlike the regular gum you tried, these actually contain nicotine. The idea is that every time you feel the urge to smoke, you use a piece of nicotine gum instead. If you're currently at about 15 cigarettes a day, we'd start you on the 2 mg strength. Over the weeks, we gradually reduce the number of pieces you use each day — maybe starting at 15, then reducing to 12, then 10, and so on — until your body no longer depends on nicotine."[5][4]

> **Dentist:** "Now, I'd also like to ask — do you feel there are any specific situations or triggers that make it hard for you to stop? For example, some patients find they smoke more in social groups at work, or when they're stressed — does anything like that apply to you?"

> **Patient:** "Not really. It's just been so many years — it's become a habit more than anything else."

> **Dentist:** "That's very understandable. Long-term habits can be really difficult to break, but the good news is it absolutely can be done with the right support and plan."

> **Dentist:** "I also have some printed materials here about NRT that I'd love to give you to take home. Our whole team is familiar with this information and can answer any questions you have along the way."[6]

***

### A5 — ARRANGE: Action Plan & Follow-Up

> **Dentist:** "So let's put together a plan together. I'd like to involve you in setting a timeline that feels realistic and achievable. Do you think you'd be comfortable aiming to be completely free of smoking within the next four to six months?"

> **Patient:** "Yes, that sounds reasonable."

> **Dentist:** "Perfect. Let's set that as our target date. Here's what I'd suggest: start with the nicotine patch — put one on tomorrow morning. Come back and see me in one week so we can check in on how you're feeling, whether you're experiencing any withdrawal symptoms, and adjust the plan if needed."[3]

> **Dentist:** "As we move forward, we'll gradually reduce the nicotine dose — stepping down the patch strength every few weeks — until you no longer need it. I'll also keep tracking how your asthma and breathing are doing, because we should start to see some improvement there as well."

> **Dentist:** "And one last thing — if at some point the OTC NRT alone isn't enough, there are prescription-based medications that a physician can prescribe. I'd be happy to refer you to your doctor at that stage if needed, and we'll work together as a team."[7]

> **Dentist:** "Does all of this sound good to you? Do you have any questions at all?"

> **Patient:** "No, this is very helpful. Thank you so much."

> **Dentist:** "You're very welcome. I'm really proud of you for taking this step, and I'll be right here supporting you every step of the way."

***

## Script at a Glance

| Step | Key Action | Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|
| **ASK** | Duration, cpd, tobacco type, prior attempts | Always quantify smoking history |
| **ADVISE** | Clear, personalized statement about risk | Keep it empathetic, not judgmental |
| **ASSESS** | Readiness: within 6 months? Within 1 month? | Only proceed to NRT if within 1 month [8] |
| **Medical Hx** | Allergies, asthma, breathing problems | Link asthma worsening to smoking [3] |
| **ASSIST** | Explain why gum failed; introduce patch + NRT gum | Explain tapering mechanism [4] |
| **ARRANGE** | Set quit date, follow-up in 1 week, offer booklet | Refer to physician if OTC NRT insufficient [7] |

Sources
[1] Interventions for Smoking Cessation and Treatments ... - NCBI https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK555596/
[2] [PDF] Counseling Your Patients about Tobacco Cessation: The 5A's https://www.sfhp.org/wp-content/files/Counseling-Your-Patients-about-Tobacco-Cessation.pdf
[3] TOBACCO CESSATION GUIDANCE https://www.fdiworlddental.org/sites/default/files/2021-07/FDI%20Tobacco%20Cessation%20Guide%20160621.pdf
[4] Tobacco Use Cessation Services and the Role of the ... https://www.cdha.ca/pdfs/profession/resources/1004_tobacco.pdf
[5] Dispensing of Nicotine Replacement Therapy (NRT) https://www.youcanmakeithappen.ca/wp-content/uploads/2014/10/DirectiveDispensingNRTNov2011Final.pdf
[6] A Practical Guide to Tobacco Cessation in Dental Offices https://www.cda-adc.ca/jcda/vol-67/issue-3/153.pdf
[7] Situational Judgement Preparation Course For NDECC https://www.itdonline.ca/courses/situational-judgement-for-ndecc
[8] Five Major Steps to Intervention (The "5 A's") https://www.ahrq.gov/prevention/guidelines/tobacco/5steps.html
[9] ® https://ndeb-bned.ca/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/NDECC-Protocol_Effective-September-30-2024.pdf
[10] Everything about NDEB's NDECC situational judgement exam https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ymjsBR2-occ
[11] NDECC​® - The National Dental Examining Board of ... https://ndeb-bned.ca/equivalency-process/ndecc-2/
[12] Situational Judgement - Foundations For NDECC Exam https://www.itdonline.ca/courses/situational-judgement-cases-scenarios-for-ndecc-exam
[13] NDECC SITUATIONAL JUDGEMENT EXAM - Full course and Mock exam https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AnYi3dsQJU0
[14] FREE First Lecture | NDECC Situational Judgement Course | NDEB Equivalency https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1B7Xwpkn2KQ
[15] Course | Situational Judgment https://passingplandental.ca/Courses/CourseDetail/3
[16] NDECC® Situational Judgement Full Course - Prep Doctors https://ca.prepdoctors.com/courses/ndeb-dental-equivalency/ndecc-situational-judgement
[17] NDECC® Situational Judgement Fundamentals https://ca.prepdoctors.com/courses/ndeb-dental-equivalency/ndecc-situational-judgement-fundamentals

title: Incomplete rct
content:
Got it — you want a **professionally organized version** of this long scenario for **NDECC/SJ-style dental exam revision**, focusing on communication, professionalism, and clinical reasoning. Below is a **clear, structured version** of your scenario and approach that you can study and practice.

***

## Scenario Summary

**Situation:**  
A patient whom you haven’t seen in **3 years** presents to your clinic. Two months ago, they had tried to contact your clinic but it was closed for the holidays. They then went to an **emergency clinic** for pain on a tooth, where they were told **root canal treatment (RCT)** was done. They now return asking for a **crown** on that same tooth.

**Findings:**  
A **radiograph** taken today shows an **incomplete root canal treatment**.

**Task:**  
You must **discuss your findings** with the patient in a **professional, objective, and empathetic way**—without criticizing or speculating about the other dentist’s work.

***

## Key Professionalism Focus

This station evaluates your ability to:
- Demonstrate **professional communication** with patients.
- Handle **discrepancies between patient expectations and clinical findings** tactfully.
- Remain **objective, respectful, and patient-centered**.
- Manage **ethical boundaries** — avoid making negative or speculative remarks about another clinician.

***

## Step-by-Step Approach

### 1. Opening and Rapport-Building
- Greet the patient warmly and confirm their understanding of today’s appointment.

> “Hello, it’s nice to see you again after such a long time. I understand you’d like to get a crown for your tooth today.”

- Confirm the patient’s version of events.

> “Could you tell me a bit more about what was done previously on that tooth?”

Patient responds: they had pain, went to an emergency dentist, got a root canal done, and were told to follow up for a crown.

***

### 2. Presenting Your Findings Objectively
Acknowledge their history, then share your findings **without judgment** and **without labeling** previous work as incomplete or poor.

> “Thank you for sharing that. Based on the x-ray I took today, it looks like your tooth still requires some additional treatment before we can proceed with the crown.”

Avoid saying “the root canal was done incorrectly” or “it’s incomplete.”  
Use neutral language such as:
- “needs further work”
- “requires completion”
- “still needs some more cleaning or filling”

***

### 3. Explain the Clinical Implication
Clarify why the crown cannot be placed immediately and what must be done next.

> “Before a crown can be placed, the inside of the tooth needs to be fully sealed to prevent reinfection. From what I see on your x-ray, we’ll need to complete that step first to ensure your tooth is healthy and strong for the crown.”

If straightforward, you may complete the RCT yourself.  
If complicated (e.g., open canal >2 months, possible perforation, etc.), explain need for a specialist:

> “Given how long the tooth has been open, it would be best for a specialist (endodontist) to complete the treatment first, and I can then place the crown.”

***

### 4. Handling Patient Questions Professionally
If the patient expresses confusion or frustration:

> “That’s a good question, and I completely understand your concern. Since I wasn’t present during your previous appointment, I can’t comment on what was discussed or done at that time. I can only share what I’m seeing today and guide you on the next steps.”

Redirect gently if patient insists:

> “If you have any questions about the previous procedure, I’d encourage you to contact that clinic—they’ll be able to clarify what was done. For now, I’ll focus on ensuring your tooth gets the complete treatment it needs.”

***

### 5. Outline the Next Steps
Summarize clearly what will happen next:
- Complete/redo the RCT, or refer to a specialist.
- Once the RCT is finished, proceed with the crown.

> “So to move forward safely, we’ll first complete the treatment inside the tooth. Once that’s done, we’ll take another x-ray to confirm it’s sealed properly and then plan for your crown.”

***

### 6. Maintain Professional Tone Throughout

**Be empathetic and calm:**  
- Avoid blaming or speculation.
- Avoid judging the patient for missed appointments.
- Avoid judging the previous dentist’s decisions.

**Correct Response Style:**
> “Based on the image from today…”  
> “The tooth appears to need a bit more treatment…”  
> “I was not present for that procedure, so I can’t comment…”  
> “Here’s what we can do from this point forward…”

**Avoid Critical Errors:**
- Criticizing the other dentist (“They didn’t do it properly.”)
- Agreeing blindly with the patient and proceeding to crown.
- Showing frustration or sarcasm.
- Making assumptions about negligence.

***

## Key Phrases for Simulation

**Acknowledging the patient:**
> “Thank you for updating me on what happened earlier.”

**Objectively presenting findings:**
> “From the image I’ve taken today, I can see that this tooth still needs additional internal treatment.”

**Providing rationale:**
> “To ensure long-term success and prevent reinfection, this step needs to be completed before placing a crown.”

**Deflecting inappropriate comparison:**
> “As I wasn’t present then, I’m unable to comment on the previous treatment decisions.”

**Redirecting focus:**
> “What’s important now is getting this tooth ready for the crown safely and effectively.”

***

## Summary Table for Quick Revision

| Step | What to Do | Example Phrase |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| 1. Greeting & history | Build rapport, gather background | “I understand you’re here for a crown. Could you tell me what was done before?” |
| 2. Present findings | Stay objective and neutral | “On today’s x-ray, it looks like this tooth still needs a bit more work.” |
| 3. Explain significance | Link to patient goal | “We need to finish the treatment before placing the crown to protect the tooth.” |
| 4. Handle concerns | Stay professional, don’t speculate | “Since I wasn’t present then, I can’t comment, but I can explain what we need to do now.” |
| 5. Plan next steps | Outline treatment or referral | “We’ll complete the root treatment, then proceed with the crown.” |
| 6. Maintain professionalism | Kind, nonjudgmental tone | “I appreciate your patience as we get this done properly.” |

***

Would you like me to write a **sample dialogue** (patient-dentist conversation) following this structured approach for your mock exam 


Below is a **ready-to-practice sample dialogue** you can use for NDECC SJ-style preparation. It follows the scenario you described and emphasizes professionalism, neutrality, and clear communication.  

***

## Sample Dialogue (Dentist–Patient)

**Dentist enters, greets, builds rapport**

**Dentist:**  
Hi, Mr. Smith, it’s nice to see you again after a long time. I understand you’re here today to get a crown on this tooth, is that right?[1]

**Patient:**  
Yes, this tooth was hurting a couple of months ago. I went to a walk-in clinic and they did a root canal. They told me I need a crown, so I came back here to get it done.

**Dentist (acknowledge and invite story):**  
Thank you for explaining that to me. Just so I’m clear, you had pain, went to the walk‑in clinic, they treated the tooth and told you a crown was needed, and now you’re here to have that crown done. Is that correct?[1]

**Patient:**  
Yes, exactly.

***

### Presenting your findings objectively

**Dentist:**  
I appreciate you sharing that. I took an x‑ray of your tooth today, and I’d like to show you what I see.[2][3]

**Patient:**  
Okay.

**Dentist (neutral, non‑critical wording):**  
If you look here at this area on the x‑ray, you can see the inside of the root where the treatment is done. From what I see, your tooth still needs some additional work inside before we can safely place a crown.[3][2]

**Patient:**  
What do you mean? They told me the root canal was done.

**Dentist (objective, not blaming):**  
I understand that’s what you were told, and I can see why this is confusing. What I can tell you is that, based on the x‑ray I took today, the tooth is not yet fully sealed inside. That means we need to do a bit more treatment to clean and fill the root completely, so the tooth is well protected before we put a crown on it.[2][3]

***

### Explaining why more work is needed

**Patient:**  
So you can’t just do the crown today?

**Dentist:**  
To give you the best long‑term result, it’s important that the inside of the tooth is fully treated first. If we put a crown on a tooth that hasn’t been completely treated, there is a higher risk of infection or problems later on. Finishing the treatment inside first helps us protect your tooth and your investment in the crown.[4][3][2]

***

### When the patient questions the previous dentist

**Patient (testing you):**  
Why didn’t the other dentist tell me this? Did they do something wrong?

**Dentist (acknowledge feeling, stay neutral):**  
That’s a very understandable question, and I can see why you’re concerned.[3][4]
Since I wasn’t there during that appointment, I’m not able to comment on what was discussed or what decisions were made at that time. Different dentists may have different approaches or may have planned to see you again to continue the treatment. What I can do is explain clearly what I’m seeing today and what we need to do from this point forward.[5][6][7]

**Patient:**  
So you’re not saying they messed up?

**Dentist:**  
I’m not in a position to judge another dentist’s treatment because I don’t know all the details of that visit. My responsibility is to give you an honest picture of your current situation and guide you on the safest next steps. If you have questions about what was done there, you absolutely have the right to contact that clinic and ask for clarification.[6][7][5]

***

### Offering a clear plan

**Patient:**  
Okay… so what happens now?

**Dentist (offer options and plan):**  
Based on what I see now, there are two main options.[1][3]

1. I can complete the treatment inside the tooth here—finish the cleaning and filling of the root—and once that’s done and looks good on the x‑ray, we go ahead with the crown.  
2. If the tooth is more complex than it appears, or if you prefer, I can refer you to an endodontist, a root‑canal specialist, to complete the treatment first. After they finish, you return here and we place the crown.[8][2][3]

In either case, the first step is to complete the internal treatment, and the second step is the crown you came in for.[3][1]

**Patient:**  
Which option do you recommend?

**Dentist:**  
From what I see right now, I believe we can likely manage this here and complete the treatment for you. If, once I start, I find anything more complicated, I will discuss it with you and we can decide together if a specialist referral is better. My goal is to make sure the tooth is treated thoroughly and that your crown lasts as long as possible.[4][2][3]

***

### Checking understanding and consent

**Dentist:**  
How does that sound to you? Do you have any questions or concerns about what I’ve explained so far?[1][3]

**Patient:**  
No, that makes sense. I just wish I’d known earlier.

**Dentist (empathy and reassurance):**  
I understand it’s frustrating to hear there’s more work needed when you thought it was finished. The good news is that we’ve caught it now, and we can take the proper steps to get this tooth healthy and ready for a crown. We’ll go at a pace you’re comfortable with, and I’ll explain each step as we go.[2][4][3]

**Patient:**  
Okay, let’s go ahead and do what’s needed.

**Dentist:**  
Great. We’ll start by scheduling the appointment to complete the treatment inside the tooth, and then we’ll plan for your crown afterwards. If any questions come up later, please feel free to ask me at any time.[3][1]

***

If you’d like, I can also create a **short bullet-point script** you can memorize (opening, findings, handling criticism, closing) for rapid recall during the exam.

Sources
[1] Beyond The Basics: A Deep Dive Into NDECC's Situational Judgement https://www.wisesouls.ca/post/judgement-day-a-deep-dive-into-ndecc-s-situational-judgement
[2] Patient Communication in Root Canal Therapy https://learn.globalsurgical.com/blog/patient-communication-in-root-canal-therapy
[3] An Overview of Dentist–Patient Communication in Quality Dental ... https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11763373/
[4] 5 Root Canal Treatment Insights for Better Patient Dentist ... https://dentalcarefree.com/blogs/5-Root-Canal-Treatment-Insights-for-Better-Patient-Dentist-Communication.html
[5] Help! A New Patient Is Complaining About Their Previous Dentist https://www.profsolutions.com/industries/dentists/insurance/risk-management/help-a-new-patient-is-complaining-about-their-previous-dentist/
[6] One of the most difficult issues you will encounter in your daily practice is addressing a patient’s complaint https://dentistcare.com/globalassets/optimized-pdfs/bulletins/justifiable-criticism.pdf
[7] How to Deal with Another Dentist's Mistake - The Dawson Academy https://thedawsonacademy.com/how-to-deal-with-another-dentists-mistake/
[8] How to Use Root Canal Success Strategies for Enhanced Communication - Dentalcarefree https://dentalcarefree.com/blogs/How-to-Use-Root-Canal-Success-Strategies-for-Enhanced-Communication.html
[9] Everything about NDEB's NDECC situational judgement exam https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ymjsBR2-occ
[10] Situational Judgement Test Practice Paper https://www.copdend.org/wp-content/uploads/2018/08/DFT-Situational-Judgement-Test-Scenarios.pdf

title: Community grant for snacks
content:
Here’s a cleaned-up, exam-style outline you can memorize and adapt in the ND(E)CC station.

***

## 1. Opening and Information Gathering

1. Greet and appreciate  
   - “Thank you for coming, this is **wonderful** for the community.”  
   - Link oral health to general health and your role as dental professional/health promoter.

2. Clarify target audience  
   - “Who exactly are you targeting: young children, adolescents, adults, geriatrics, or a mix?”  
   - “Are there specific groups: school children, sports clubs, long‑term care residents, pregnant women, etc.?”

3. Clarify logistics and constraints  
   - “Is this a one‑time grant or ongoing funding?”  
   - “Do you already have:  
     - Any contracts with snack suppliers/school canteens/vending machines?  
     - Volunteers or staff to help run activities?”  
   - “Do you have any existing channels to reach people: schools, senior centers, sports clubs, social media?”

4. Clarify communication tools  
   - “How are you planning to get information out: posters, flyers, workshops, school talks, social media, health fairs, snack tasting booths?”  
   - If they say “I don’t know yet”, acknowledge and say you’ll suggest a structured plan and examples.

Then say: “I’ll first give you a general plan that works for most populations, then we can tailor for your specific groups.”

***

## 2. Core Health Messages (General Principles)

Explain in simple language:

- Frequent sugars and refined carbs → more acid attacks → higher caries risk.[1][2][3][4]
- Tooth‑friendly foods are:  
  - Low in added sugar, not sticky, not slowly dissolving in the mouth.[5]
  - Higher in fiber or protein and require chewing (stimulate saliva).[6][7]
- Best beverages for teeth are plain water and plain milk; sugary and acidic drinks are harmful.[7][8]

Use one simple example:  
- “Sipping juice or pop all afternoon is much worse than having a small dessert once with a meal.”

***

## 3. Tooth-Friendly Snacks: What to Recommend

Emphasize: crunchy, low sugar, non‑sticky, saliva‑stimulating, nutrient‑dense.[5][6][7]

### A. Recommended snacks

1. Fresh fruits (whole, not juice)  
   - Apples, pears, berries, watermelon, etc. (emphasize whole fruit, not dried or juiced).[6][7]
   - High water and fiber content help “self‑clean” teeth.

2. Crunchy vegetables  
   - Carrots, celery, cucumbers, bell peppers, cherry tomatoes.[7][6]
   - “Natural toothbrush effect”: they help disrupt plaque and stimulate saliva.

3. Dairy products (unsweetened)  
   - Cheese cubes/sticks, plain yogurt, plain kefir.[9][1][6][7]
   - Provide calcium and phosphorus, help neutralize acid, form a protective environment for enamel.

4. Protein‑rich snacks  
   - Nuts and seeds (if allergy‑safe), boiled eggs, hummus with veggie sticks, tofu pieces, beans/lentil snacks.[9][5][6]
   - Filling, reduce frequent sugar snacking.

5. Whole‑grain, low‑sugar options  
   - Whole‑grain crackers or toast with cheese, small whole‑grain sandwiches with lean protein, low‑sugar homemade muffins, oats.[1][5][9]

6. Popcorn (special case)  
   - Plain, air‑popped, unflavoured popcorn as a better movie snack than candy and caramel popcorn.[9]
   - Warn about kernels getting stuck and the need for good brushing.

### B. Snacks to limit or avoid

Explain both caries risk and stickiness/frequency.[2][4][5]

- Sticky, long‑lasting sweets: toffees, caramels, fruit roll‑ups, gummies, sticky granola bars.[4][5][7][9]
- Highly refined starchy snacks that stick: chips, crackers, pretzels, nachos, sweet muffins.[5][7][9]
- Sweetened yogurts and flavored dairy: “fruit‑on‑the‑bottom”, drinking yogurts with high sugar.[4][1]
- Dried fruits and fruit leathers (very concentrated sugar and sticky).[4][5][9]

Reinforce:  
- “If they do have these, it’s safer with a main meal instead of as frequent between‑meal snacks.”[2][5]

***

## 4. Tooth-Friendly Beverages: What to Recommend

Make sure you cover beverages clearly, since that was a common omission.

### A. Recommended beverages

1. Plain water  
   - First choice at all ages; fluoridated tap water if available.[8][7]
   - Between meals and snacks, water should be the only routine drink.

2. Plain milk (depending on audience)  
   - Especially for children and some older adults needing calcium/protein.[8][1][7][9]
   - Emphasize: not chocolate, not flavored, no added sugar or syrups.

3. Unsweetened homemade smoothies (occasional, with rules)  
   - Made at home with whole fruits/vegetables, no added sugar/honey/syrups.  
   - Advise to have them with meals, not sipped over long periods.

### B. Beverages to limit or avoid

Highlight both sugar and acidity.[3][2][7][8][4]

- Soft drinks/pop, energy drinks, and sports drinks.  
- Fruit juices (even “100% juice”) and fruit punches.  
- Sweetened iced teas, flavored waters, vitamin waters with sugar.  
- Flavored milks, milkshakes, hot chocolate mixes with sugar.  
- Sugary coffees and bubble teas.

Key phrasing:  
- “Even 100% fruit juice is still concentrated sugar. It is much better to eat the fruit and drink water.”[3][1][2][4]

Also stress frequency:  
- “Sipping sugary or acidic drinks over hours is especially damaging, because teeth are under constant acid attack.”[2][3][4]

***

## 5. Behaviour and Timing Advice

Short, exam‑friendly points:

- Try to keep sugar‑containing foods and drinks to main mealtimes.[2][5]
- Avoid “grazing” or constant sipping between meals.[3][4][2]
- Encourage water after any snack to rinse the mouth.[7][8]
- Combine diet advice with brushing twice daily with fluoride toothpaste.[1]

***

## 6. How to Communicate With the Community

Give them an action plan and show that you can support beyond just listing foods.

### A. Targeted materials

Recommend a targeted plan:

- Divide by audience:  
  - Children/adolescents (schools, sports teams).  
  - Adults (workplaces, general community events).  
  - Older adults (long‑term care, senior clubs).  
- Adjust examples and visuals to each age group (cartoons for kids, simple infographics for adults, large‑print for seniors).

### B. Channels and tools

Suggest options they can choose from:

- Posters and infographics in the community center, schools, clinics, sports halls.  
- Simple flyers or fridge‑friendly “tooth‑friendly snack lists” for parents and caregivers.  
- Short talks or workshops at schools, parent meetings, or senior centers.  
- Snack “tasting” booths at community events, with tooth‑friendly options and quick explanations.  
- Social media posts with one key message each (e.g., “Water is the best drink for your teeth”).

### C. Key messages to print or repeat

Advise them to repeat a small set of simple messages everywhere:

- “Choose crunch: fruits, veggies, cheese, nuts.”  
- “Water and milk are the best drinks for your teeth.”  
- “Save sweets and sugary drinks for special occasions and have them with meals, not all day.”  
- “Rinse with water and brush with fluoride toothpaste twice a day.”

Offer collaboration:  
- “I’d be happy to help review your posters or handouts, or even come to a school or community session to talk about tooth‑friendly snacks and drinks.”

***

## 7. How to Close in the Station

End with a brief, structured wrap‑up:

- Reassure them and summarize:  
  - You will:  
    - Help them define target groups.  
    - Promote tooth‑friendly snacks (crunchy fruits/veggies, unsweetened dairy, nuts, whole grains).  
    - Promote tooth‑friendly beverages (mainly water, plus plain milk for those who need it).  
    - Avoid sugary, sticky snacks and sugary/acidic drinks.  
    - Support them in creating targeted posters, flyers, and activities.  
- Invite future contact: “If you’d like, we can plan separate materials for kids versus adults once you decide which group you’re focusing on first.”


Here is a detailed, realistic role-play script designed specifically for your NDECC Situational Judgement practice. 

To help you prepare for different directions the actor might take, I have divided the script into the core phases of the consultation. 

***

### **Phase 1: Introduction & Information Gathering**
*(Goal: Build rapport, validate the initiative, and uncover missing details before giving advice.)*

**Candidate (You):** "Hi Sarah, it is so great to see you today! I understand you have some exciting news regarding the local community center?"

**Patient (Actor):** "Yes, Dr. Chan! We just received a government grant to optimize healthy snacks and beverages for the community. Since I'm the lead volunteer, I immediately thought of coming to you for guidance on what we should offer."

**Candidate (You):** "That is fantastic news, congratulations! It is wonderful that the center is taking this initiative, because oral health plays such a massive role in our overall, general health. I would be absolutely delighted to help you with this."

**Patient (Actor):** "Thank you! So, what kind of snacks do you think we should buy?"

**Candidate (You):** "I have some great options in mind, but before we dive into the specific foods, could you tell me a little bit more about the target audience? Are we focusing mostly on young children, teenagers, seniors, or just the general public?"

**Patient (Actor):** *(Standardized Patient trap: giving no information)* "To be honest, we haven't finalized that yet. The grant just came through, and I haven't really put together a concrete plan. I was hoping you could just give me some general input to start."

**Candidate (You):** "Not a problem at all! Since we are targeting a broad community, we can discuss some universally tooth-friendly options and how to safely present them. Does that sound like a good plan?"

**Patient (Actor):** "Yes, that sounds perfect."

***

### **Phase 2: Explaining the "Why" & Snack Recommendations**
*(Goal: Avoid jargon while explaining the mechanics of tooth-friendly foods, and give specific examples).*

**Candidate (You):** "When we look at foods that are good for our teeth, we want to focus on two things: foods that are naturally low in sugar, and foods that are crunchy or chewy. Crunchy foods act almost like nature's toothbrush—they help physically sweep away plaque and stimulate saliva, which naturally protects the teeth. Does that make sense?"

**Patient (Actor):** "Oh, I never thought about it like that. So what would be some good examples?"

**Candidate (You):** "Great examples are fresh, crunchy vegetables like carrots, celery, and cucumbers. We also highly recommend cheese. Cheese is fantastic because it's rich in calcium and phosphorus, which actually help rebuild the tooth enamel. Plain, unsweetened yogurt is another excellent source of calcium."

**Patient (Actor):** "Okay, vegetables, cheese, yogurt. What about granola bars or fruit roll-ups? The kids usually love those."

**Candidate (You):** "I'm glad you brought those up. We actually want to avoid sticky foods like fruit roll-ups, gummies, or even certain granola bars. They contain high amounts of refined sugars and stick inside the deep grooves of the teeth for hours, which can quickly lead to cavities. Similarly, we want to avoid refined carbohydrates like plain crackers or chips, which turn into a sticky paste in the mouth."

**Patient (Actor):** "That makes sense. What about nuts or popcorn?"

**Candidate (You):** "Unflavored popcorn is a great whole-grain option, just make sure to avoid the caramel or maple syrup-coated ones. Nuts are incredibly healthy and tooth-friendly, however, because you are serving a broad Canadian community, we must be very mindful of severe nut allergies. If you decide to provide nuts, I highly recommend keeping them in separate, clearly labeled packaging."

***

### **Phase 3: The Beverage Trap**
*(Goal: Seamlessly transition to beverages, ensuring you do not miss this critical part of the prompt).*

**Patient (Actor):** "Okay, this is a great list for snacks. I think I have enough to go to the grocery store now."

**Candidate (You):** "That's a great start for snacks, but remember, the grant also covers beverages, which are just as critical for oral health! What types of drinks were you considering?"

**Patient (Actor):** "Oh right, drinks! Well, I figured 100% natural fruit juice like apple or orange juice. That's healthy, right?"

**Candidate (You):** "It is a very common misconception! While eating a raw apple is great because of the crunch and the fiber, apple *juice* has all the fiber removed and leaves behind highly concentrated sugar. Even if it says 100% natural, that liquid sugar bathes the teeth and significantly increases the risk of cavities. So, we strongly advise against fruit juices, sports drinks, and soda."

**Patient (Actor):** "Wow, I didn't realize juice was that bad for teeth. What should we offer instead?"

**Candidate (You):** "The absolute best beverage you can offer is plain water. It has zero calories, neutralizes acids, and helps wash away food particles. If you know there will be a lot of growing children or seniors at the center, offering plain, unflavored milk is also a wonderful option because of the calcium. Just ensure it isn't chocolate or strawberry milk, as those contain a lot of added sugar."

***

### **Phase 4: Implementation & Health Promotion**
*(Goal: Fulfill the requirement of guiding them on how to convey this information).*

**Patient (Actor):** "This is so helpful. Do you have any advice on how to get the community on board with this? Sometimes people just want junk food."

**Candidate (You):** "That is a very valid point, change can take time. Since you have a grant, I would recommend using a small portion of it for education. We could put up colorful, easy-to-read posters near the snack stations explaining *why* these foods make them stronger and healthier. You could also use 'Tooth-Friendly' green stickers on the healthy options to make it a fun choice for the kids."

**Patient (Actor):** "I love the sticker idea! And posters would be really easy to print out."

**Candidate (You):** "Exactly! And please know you aren't alone in this. I would be more than happy to review your final shopping list before you make any big purchases, or even come down to the center to give a fun, 10-minute presentation to the community if you'd like."

**Patient (Actor):** "Dr. Chan, that would be amazing. Thank you so much for your time today."

**Candidate (You):** "It's my pleasure, Sarah. I'm really proud of the work you're doing for our community. Let's keep in touch on how the rollout goes!"

***

### **Practice Tips for this Script:**
*   **Pacing:** Do not rush through the "Do's and Don'ts" list. Pause after explaining the *why* (e.g., saliva stimulation) to let the actor acknowledge it.
*   **Handling Interruptions:** Standardized patients are trained to interrupt or ask naive questions (like the juice example). Welcome these interruptions smoothly ("I'm so glad you brought that up...").
*   **Empathy & Tone:** Keep your tone encouraging rather than lecturing. Use phrases like "We highly recommend" instead of "You must do this."
